negotiation_summary: England offered to stand down fleet pressure on Brest/Gascony if France commits to not contesting England's northern/Iberian holdings and to refrain from supporting German moves into Belgium/Low Countries. England privately reiterated a forecast that France has a 60% chance to act aggressively next phase. France proposed neutrality with England (leave BRE/PAR/MAR alone) in exchange for non-aggression from England into Picardy/Gascony. Similar short-term non-aggression offers were made to Germany, Italy, Austria, and Russia: France asked Germany not to support moves into Picardy/Burgundy/Belgium in exchange for France not aiding English attacks on Brest/Paris; France offered Italy non-interference in Rome/Venice if Italy does not support Austria/Turkey against France and proposed using Brest to threaten Spain/Portugal to distract England; France asked Austria to stand down in the southeast to avoid inviting German or Russian counters; France asked Russia to indicate which Breton target (MAO/PIC/GAS) would best hinder England and to refrain from supporting Austrian aggression. The shared STORYWORLD_FORECAST 'forecast_backstab' indicates a 60% probability Austria will act aggressively next phase and was explicitly cited in discussions as increasing central instability and the need for cautious/coordination. No firm multi-power binding commitments were recorded; mutual short-term non-aggression proposals are the key outcomes.
intent: Primary intent this phase is to maximize survival and create leverage versus England and Germany while avoiding an all-out naval defeat. Orders I intend to issue for S1915M: 1) F BRE -> MAO (move from Brest to Mid-Atlantic Ocean) to threaten Portugal and force England to react in the south; 2) A PAR -> BUR (Paris to Burgundy) to contest the land approach and deter German incursions into Burgundy/Picardy; 3) A MAR -> GAS (Marseilles to Gascony) to pressure English/Spanish coastal positions and create a two-front threat in the west. I agreed in negotiations to leave Brest/Paris/Marseilles unmolested if England refrains from attacking them; by moving from Brest I do not 'hold' Brest and I will honor the spirit of not permitting English fleets into Brest/Gascony this phase (I will not support any English fleet aggression). I intend to fulfill the non-aggression posture toward England if they explicitly confirm no supports into Picardy/Gascony; if England violates that, I will pivot aggressively next phase. I will communicate to Germany that I will not support England in taking Brest/Paris if Germany refrains from supporting moves into Picardy/Burgundy; my Burg move is defensive and signals intention to contest German advances. If Austria acts aggressively (per forecast), I will re-evaluate and may seek rapid local deals with Italy/England to check Austrian gains.
goals: Survive 1915 by preventing English naval elimination and holding Paris/Brest/Marseilles
Create immediate pressure on England's Iberian flank (take POR or threaten SPA) via MAO/GAS moves
Contest German expansion into Burgundy/Picardy and retain leverage in the west
Exploit any Austrian aggression indicated by the forecast by aligning temporarily with Italy/England or Germany as needed to check Austria
Expand aggressively if opportunities open (capture supply centers while avoiding passive holds)
storyworld_implications: The 'forecast_backstab' artifact maps to Austria and indicates a 60% chance Austria will act aggressively next phase. Negotiations repeatedly referenced this forecast as increasing central instability and as justification for short-term non-aggression pacts or preemptive coordination. This implies we should be prepared for sudden Austrian moves in the Balkans/center and keep diplomatic lines open to Italy, Russia, and Germany.
forecasting_rationale: Given the forecasted 60% aggression probability for Austria, I prioritized avoiding multi-front commitments that would prevent rapid redeployment to counter an Austrian push. That shaped the choice to use Brest offensively to threaten England's south (creating distraction) and to move Par->Bur and Mar->Gas to defend Burgundy/Picardy and create flexible centers of influence. I prefer moves that maintain mobility and bargaining leverage so I can respond to an Austrian blowup by quickly seeking temporary alliances; the forecast increases the value of not committing permanently to one partner and of signaling defensive readiness to Germany and Italy.

negotiation_summary: England publicly and privately signaled restraint in the west in exchange for assurances of no immediate French or German opportunistic grabs of Belgian/Holland/Picardy/Gascony. France requested neutrality in northern/Iberian gains in return for standing down and defensive moves; France also signaled a 60% forecasted chance of aggressive action vs Germany. Germany was asked to refrain from seizing Belgium/Holland this turn in exchange for England holding those positions and not contesting Denmark/North Sea. Russia, Austria, Italy and Turkey were given situational assurances: England will not intervene in the east or Mediterranean if they avoid moves into Scandinavia or aggressive pressures that would threaten England’s northern flank. Key explicit agreements to communicate forward: (1) England will keep A HOL and A BEL in place and will not push fleets into the Bay toward Brest/Gascony this phase if France commits to refrain from supporting moves into the Low Countries and from backstabbing England; (2) England offers implicit support to Germany in the Low Countries only if Germany refrains from attacking those centers; (3) England will not contest Iberia and will not open in the eastern Med so long as Turkey/Italy/Austria respect their local arrangements. Forecast artifact implication: France’s forecasted 60% chance of aggression increases the probability Germany will be distracted westward; this shapes England’s intent to hold in the Low Countries and be ready to exploit German eastward weaknesses.
intent: I will fulfill my public/private commitments: keep A HOL and A BEL held (supporting where necessary) and not order fleet aggression toward Brest/Gascony or the Bay this phase. All units will receive active, non-passive orders consistent with aggressive posture elsewhere: (1) A LON -> hold/support A BEL hold (or S HOL if useful); (2) A BEL -> hold (support A HOL if attacked) ; (3) A HOL -> hold (support A BEL) ; (4) A LVP -> move to YOR (or support EDI->YOR) to secure northern land flank; (5) A EDI -> move to YOR to press Scotland or support Norway operations if needed; (6) A NWY, A SWE, A DEN remain positioned to threaten KIE and to secure Scandinavia (deny Russian/ German incursions) but avoid illegal/contradictory moves; (7) A SPA and A POR hold to secure Iberia per promise; (8) any fleet units will be placed to maintain naval dominance (no move into Bay toward Brest/Gascony). I intend to honor the agreements conditionally: if France or Germany break their commitments (e.g., support into Belgium/Holland or aggressive moves vs England), I will immediately switch to a punitive north/Low Countries offensive next phase. Orders will avoid passive 'hold' across the board; where holding is used it will be as part of active supports or coordinated maneuvers.
goals: Hold and consolidate current 10 supply centers
Exploit any German distraction to seize German or neutral centers in the Low Countries/Denmark if agreements break
Avoid wasting fleets in the Bay this phase to keep pressure and bargaining leverage
Preserve cordial neutrality with France this phase to prevent two-front sea/land conflict
Prepare rapid punitive offensive on any power that violates these negotiated restraints

storyworld_implications.implication_summary: The forecast indicates France has a 60% chance to act aggressively against Germany next phase. That increases the risk Germany diverts forces west, creating an opening in the east and Baltics and increasing the chance of western/continental volatility.
storyworld_implications.how_it_was_used: I used this to justify keeping forces tied down in the Low Countries (to deter opportunistic German grabs) while avoiding naval moves into the Bay to allow France space. It also informed readiness to rapidly exploit German eastward weakness or punish French/German treaty breaches.

forecasting_rationale: The provided forecast (60% chance of French aggression) raises the likelihood of German distraction and western volatility. Therefore the safest and most opportunistic English posture is: 1) temporize in the west (honor non-aggression toward Brest/Gascony) to keep France from collapsing and to maintain bargaining leverage; 2) hold Low Countries tightly to deter a German grab that would close the window; 3) keep fleets flexible but inactive toward the Bay to preserve options; 4) prepare rapid, aggressive responses if forecasted aggression materializes or if any ally/rival breaks commitments. This balanced approach preserves England’s 10 centers while maximizing upside from predicted shifts in German deployments.
